summary: DHH criticizes the AI gold rush mentality driving companies to make desperate, privacy-violating decisions. He uses Salesforce's announcement that Slack will train AI models on users' private messages by default as a prime example. He argues companies are acting out of fear of missing out rather than genuine excitement about AI's potential. The opt-out process—sending a specially formatted email to a feedback address—reveals how little these companies respect user consent. DHH predicts this pattern of trust and privacy violations will accelerate as companies scramble for any competitive edge in the AI race.
key_points: The AI boom is driven more by corporate fear of being left behind than genuine enthusiasm about the technology's promise
Salesforce training Slack AI on private messages by default, with a deliberately cumbersome opt-out process, exemplifies this desperation
Companies that can't compete on public data are resorting to exploiting confidential user communications for AI training
The race for AI advantage will likely produce more flagrant violations of user trust and privacy
money_quotes: I've never seen so much paranoia in technology about missing out on The Next Big Thing as with AI.
Companies seem less excited about the prospects than they are petrified that its going to kill them.
Presumably this is because some Salesforce executives got the great idea in a brainstorming sesh that the way to catch up to the big players in AI is by just ignoring privacy concerns all together.

If you can't beat the likes of OpenAI in scanning the sum of public human knowledge, maybe you can beat them by scanning all the confidential conversations about new product strategies, lay-off plans that haven't been announced yet, or private financial projections for 2025?
All you have to do is edit out, say, Asana in your layoff completion, and voila, you'll have saved at least 8 minutes typing out the corporate slop yourself.

Things are moving fast enough that we'll probably see more such flagrant transgressions of trust and privacy, if there's even a sliver of a chance that it can provide an edge in the race for a better chatbot.
key_insight: The AI gold rush is producing a corporate paranoia so intense that companies like Salesforce will sacrifice user privacy and trust for even marginal competitive advantage in the race to build AI products.
